Haha, thanks for the compliment (The most sweetest thing that someone could ever say to me). Although it's end of the day, this made my day.

The reason for my love for Meiyazhagan might actually be older than Meiyazhagan itself. It lies in my journey towards Meiyazhagan. 

Maybe it started when I was a child. My dad has a great narration skill tbh. He used to tell stories about his childhood, our village, how it looked back then, and even imitate people while telling abt them. I always loved listening to his stories (memories to be precise). Growing up like that, I started loving to listen when people talk about their life.

So when Meiyazhagan released, even though it had mixed reviews, I loved it. When I watched the uncut version in theatre, the “yapping” part which most people didn’t prefer, it actually gave me comfort. Until then it was just one of my favourite films of the year.

But when it came on OTT and people started talking about how the film impacted them, I loved listening to their experiences. Hearing their stories made me see Meiyazhagan in a different light. From that moment on, my love for Meiyazhagan just kept on hitting new peak!!

On every rewatch, I started to create and  relive my pleasant memories. That’s when I stopped watching it as just a film and accepted him as one of the characters in my life. Maybe that’s why I love it the way I do.

If you wanna love it like I do, just try listening to people and their experiences (Not abt Meiyazhagan, just life in general). Nothing feels as human as listening to someone narrate their life with a smile and happiness in their tone.

PS: I thought of writing a long story short, but ended up with this instead. And sorry if it feels too long!!

I’m really sorry about your grandfather. From the way you spoke about him, I can tell how deeply you loved him. He must have been a truly beautiful soul to be compared to Meiyazhagan. May his soul rest in peace.

When you said the film reminds us of our father figures, I completely agree. It doesn’t just remind us, but quietly brings back the warmth of the memories we shared with them. That kind of nostalgia feels both heavy and comforting at the same time.

“love is remembering”  that will stay with me. I don’t think being forgetful makes you any less loving. We’re humans. What truly matters is the intention to care and to try to remember. The fact that it matters to you already speaks a lot more than words.

You are absolutely right. Becoming Meiyazhagan really is the purest form of humanity. Loving without expecting, caring, Listening without Judging and remembering without being asked to. 

I’ve always believed that the most random conversations can hold the deepest meanings. Sometimes just listening to someone’s thoughts can quietly change us. I think that’s what Meiyazhagan teaches us, to slow down in a fast world and to pay attention to life and people and to hold on to memories a little tighter.

And I’m glad you’ve rewatched it countless times. It’s comforting to interact with someone who feels the same way.


You definitely love Meiyazhagan just as much as I do or maybe even more.


And if you ever feel like talking about films, memories or just life in general, I’d genuinely be happy to listen.

No need to apologise, Conversations like this don't need timing, they just need the Soul.
I'm glad that our conversation made your meiyazhagan experience better (means a lot). 

Actually, yesterday they telecast Meiyazhagan on VijaySuper. I thought of watching it, but they had trimmed a lot of scenes (I hate to watch films when they cut or skip), So I switched to Ind vs Wi match instead. 

After the First innings, I put Meiyazhagan back on, apo dhaan Arul oda confession scene start aagudhu.
Aprm enna, avlo dhaan... andha 18 mins conversation full ah pathuten. Ellam mudinchu kadaisila Arul "Kadhava thorada Meiyazhagaaa!!" Nu solrapo, ennaye ariyama clap panna start panniten. 

Although I didn't watch it completely, it gave me the satisfaction of watching it completely.

I believe that's the beauty of Meiyazhagan. It doesn't need the entire runtime, All it requires is the soulful conversations on screen like this and off screen conversations like ours to hold on to the film more tighter than ever.

It's perfect, it's perfect down to the last word 👏🏼👏🏼
